title: Nearly time-periodic water waves
speaker: Jon Wilkening
abstract: We compute new families of time-periodic and quasi-periodic solutions of the free-surface Euler equations involving extreme standing waves and collisions of traveling waves of various types. A Floquet analysis shows that many of the new solutions are linearly stable to harmonic perturbations. Evolving such perturbations (nonlinearly) over tens of thousands of cycles suggests that the solutions remain nearly time-periodic forever.

proof_stages.label: Historical singular-wave problem and numerical agenda
proof_stages.summary: Stokes' traveling-wave corner theorem and the Penny-Price/Taylor standing-wave conjecture motivate a numerical program to compute nearly singular time-periodic water waves and then test their stability and quasi-periodicity.

proof_stages.label: Boundary formulation of free-surface Euler
proof_stages.summary: The irrotational free-surface Euler equations are reduced to evolution equations for the interface height and surface velocity potential, with the nonlocal Dirichlet-Neumann operator computed by a periodic double-layer Fredholm integral equation.

proof_stages.label: Conformal-map time-stepping formulation
proof_stages.summary: An equivalent conformal-map formulation rewrites the kinematic and Bernoulli conditions in variables where Hilbert transforms supply a fast way to evolve the water-wave system.

proof_stages.label: Shooting and continuation for periodic standing waves
proof_stages.summary: Spectral spatial discretization and high-order time stepping are combined with an overdetermined Levenberg-Marquardt shooting method that drives a quarter-period velocity-potential residual to zero and then continues solutions in a Fourier-amplitude parameter.

proof_stages.label: Fragmentation instead of a limiting corner
proof_stages.summary: The computed standing-wave branches do not converge self-similarly to a 90-degree corner but fragment into many nearby branches with fine-scale oscillations, and double/quadruple precision checks support that this is not a numerical artifact.

proof_stages.label: Resonant disconnections and nonunique branches
proof_stages.summary: Branch disconnections are interpreted as resonant secondary standing waves whose appearance, disappearance, harmonic resonances, and possible Cantor-like parameterization depend strongly on fluid depth and also occur in higher-dimensional computations.

proof_stages.label: Small divisors and the infinite-depth expansion
proof_stages.summary: Implementing the Schwartz-Whitney/Plotnikov-Toland conformal expansion to high order in infinite depth suggests only exponential coefficient growth, raising the possibility that the Nash-Moser Cantor-set obstruction may not be needed in that case.

proof_stages.label: Floquet stability and nearly quasi-periodic dynamics
proof_stages.summary: Floquet multiplier computations locate stable regions and pockets of instability, while nonlinear perturbation experiments suggest long-time nearby motions with additional frequencies, connecting standing waves to traveling-standing waves and model dispersive equations.
